coredata.dc:title: Enhanced oxygen reduction electrocatalysis on PtCoSn alloy nanocatalyst mediated by Ta-doped SnO2 support for polymer electrolyte fuel cells
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2021 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Shi, Guoyu
Hashimoto, Takuma
Tryk, Donald A.
Tano, Tetsuro
Iiyama, Akihiro
Uchida, Makoto
Kakinuma, Katsuyoshi
coredata.dc:description: For polymer electrolyte fuel cells (PEFCs), platinum-based catalysts for the air cathode supported on metal oxides such as SnO2 exhibit highly promising durability. Yet, the activity for the oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) has still not been sufficiently high toward widespread application. Here, we report a new type of catalyst with significantly boosted ORR activity, based on PtCoSn alloy nanostructures formed on Ta-doped SnO2 support (PtCoSn/Ta-SnO2). The ORR activity of the PtCoSn/Ta-SnO2 catalyst is evaluated in acidic media (0.1 M HClO4) from 20 to 80 °C. The apparent rate constants (k app) for the ORR on PtCoSn/Ta-SnO2 are found to be about 4.5 and 1.5 times greater than those of commercial Pt and Pt3Co alloy catalysts supported on carbon black (c-Pt/C and c-Pt3Co/C), respectively, and increase with temperature, in accordance with an Arrhenius-type relation from 20 to 60 °C. The enhanced ORR rates of the PtCoSn/Ta-SnO2 catalyst can be correlated with an increase in the preexponential factor of the Arrhenius equation, together with a lowered activation energy. Experimental and density functional theory results reveal that the incorporation of Sn facilitates O2 adsorption and dissociation, which is beneficial to overcome the kinetic limitation of ORR. Moreover, the durability of PtCoSn/Ta-SnO2 examined by potential-step cycling between 0.6 and 1.0 V at 80 °C has been found to be much higher as compared with c-Pt/C and c-Pt3Co/C.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Polymer electrolyte fuel cell
Cathode
Oxygen reduction reaction
Electrocatalysis
Alloy

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image, (b) STEM and (c-f) corresponding EDX mapping images of PtCoSn/Ta-SnO2 catalyst.

Cyclic voltammograms for (a) PtCoSn/Ta-SnO2, (b) c-Pt3Co/C and (c) c-Pt/C catalysts in N2-saturated 0.1 M HClO4 solution (scan rate, 50 mV s−1) at different temperatures. (d) Comparison of the CVs at 60 °C for the three catalysts.

(a) ORR polarization curves in O2-saturated 0.1 M HClO4 solution at 20 °C (potential sweep rate of 5 mV s−1 and rotation rate of 1750 rpm). (b) ORR specific activity j k and mass activity MA k at 0.90 V and 20 °C. (c) Temperature dependent j k at 0.90 V. (d) Temperature dependent MA k at 0.90 V.
(a) Arrhenius plots for the apparent rate constant k app for the ORR at 0.90 V at temperatures of 20, 40, 60 and 80 °C. (b) Apparent activation energy E a at 0.90 V for the ORR.

DFT-generated atomic models for the reaction (a) O2,ad + 2H2Oad to (b) reaction products in which the O-O distance has been constrained to match the Pt-Pt distance, for Pt/Pt3(Co0.33Sn0.67)(111) (left), Pt/Pt3Co(111) (middle), and Pt(111) (right) after the transition state on the different Pt surfaces. Color code: Pt (blue), Co (purple), Sn (gray), oxygen (red) and hydrogen (white). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.).

DFT energy profiles calculated for the reaction O2,ad + 2H2Oad to a partially dissociated O2 transition state to O + 2OH + H2O on Pt/Pt3Co(111) or 2O + 2H2O on Pt/Pt3CoxSn1-x(111) (open symbols on the descending curves), and 4OHad (solid symbols on the descending curves) on Pt/Pt3Co(111) (blue circles), Pt(111) (black squares), and Pt/Pt3CoxSn1-x(111) surfaces. The Pt/Pt3CoxSn1-x(111) models include Pt/Pt3Co0.17Sn0.83(111) (red vertical diamonds), Pt/Pt3Co0.33Sn0.67(111) (light brown triangles), Pt/Pt3Co0.5Sn0.5(111) (olive green upside-down triangles), and Pt/Pt3Co0.67Sn0.33(111) (light blue horizontal diamonds). The O-O distance was used to represent the reaction coordinate, i.e., the progress of the main reaction of O2 dissociation, which also can involve protonation of the oxygen atoms. The adsorption energy being plotted is that for the O2 + 2H2O system minus that for 2H2O alone and thus signifies the stabilization of the O2 alone as it dissociates and reacts with adjacent water molecules. For reference, the adsorption energy for O2 alone, with no water present, is also plotted (leftmost points), which shows that the O2 is greatly stabilized by the presence of 2H2O. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.).

CVs and ORR polarization curves of PtCoSn/Ta-SnO2 (a,b), c-Pt3Co/C (c,d) and c-Pt/C (e,f) catalysts before and after 4000 cycles of ADT. The CVs were recorded at 80 °C in N2-saturated 0.1 M HClO4 with a scanning rate of 50 mV s−1. The ORR polarization curves were recorded at 80 °C in O2-saturated 0.1 M HClO4 at a scan rate of 5 mV s−1 and a rotation rate of 1750 rpm.
ECA (a), specific activity j k (b) and mass activity MA k (c) at 0.90 V before and after 4000 cycles of ADT at 80 °C.

head.ce:title: Enhanced oxygen reduction electrocatalysis on PtCoSn alloy nanocatalyst mediated by Ta-doped SnO2 support for polymer electrolyte fuel cells

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Fuel Cell Nanomaterials Center, University of Yamanashi, 6-43 Miyamae-cho, Kofu 400-0021, Japan
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: aFuel Cell Nanomaterials Center, University of Yamanashi, 6-43 Miyamae-cho, Kofu 400-0021, Japan

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Interdisciplinary Graduate School of Medicine, Engineering, and Agricultural Sciences, University of Yamanashi, 4 Takeda, Kofu 400-8510, Japan

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Interdisciplinary Graduate School of Medicine, Engineering, and Agricultural Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: bInterdisciplinary Graduate School of Medicine, Engineering, and Agricultural Sciences, University of Yamanashi, 4 Takeda, Kofu 400-8510, Japan

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: For polymer electrolyte fuel cells (PEFCs), platinum-based catalysts for the air cathode supported on metal oxides such as SnO2 exhibit highly promising durability. Yet, the activity for the oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) has still not been sufficiently high toward widespread application. Here, we report a new type of catalyst with significantly boosted ORR activity, based on PtCoSn alloy nanostructures formed on Ta-doped SnO2 support (PtCoSn/Ta-SnO2). The ORR activity of the PtCoSn/Ta-SnO2 catalyst is evaluated in acidic media (0.1 M HClO4) from 20 to 80 °C. The apparent rate constants (k app) for the ORR on PtCoSn/Ta-SnO2 are found to be about 4.5 and 1.5 times greater than those of commercial Pt and Pt3Co alloy catalysts supported on carbon black (c-Pt/C and c-Pt3Co/C), respectively, and increase with temperature, in accordance with an Arrhenius-type relation from 20 to 60 °C. The enhanced ORR rates of the PtCoSn/Ta-SnO2 catalyst can be correlated with an increase in the preexponential factor of the Arrhenius equation, together with a lowered activation energy. Experimental and density functional theory results reveal that the incorporation of Sn facilitates O2 adsorption and dissociation, which is beneficial to overcome the kinetic limitation of ORR. Moreover, the durability of PtCoSn/Ta-SnO2 examined by potential-step cycling between 0.6 and 1.0 V at 80 °C has been found to be much higher as compared with c-Pt/C and c-Pt3Co/C.

ce:section.ce:para: Polymer electrolyte fuel cells (PEFCs) have been recognized as a promising alternative energy source for zero-emission vehicles [1,2]. Their full-scale commercialization, however, has been hampered by the slow rate of the cathodic oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) and a critical durability issue with respect to carbon corrosion [3–6]. The Pt catalyst and high potential accelerate the corrosion rate of the carbon support, leading to agglomeration (sintering) and/or detachment of Pt nanoparticles (NPs) from the support [7]. To avoid carbon corrosion problem, a range of noncarbon support materials, such as carbides [8,9], nitrides [10,11], and especially conductive oxides [12,13] have gained much attention because of the inherent structural and chemical stability in acidic and oxidative environments. Pt electrocatalysts supported on doped tin oxides have demonstrated exceptional durability in an operating fuel cell [14–17]. We found that Pt supported on Ta-doped SnO2 (Pt/Ta-SnO2), with a fused-aggregate network microstructure, showed high ORR activity in rotating disk electrode (RDE) measurements and outstanding durability with respect to startup/shutdown operations in membrane-electrode assembly (MEA) testing, which resulted from the superb electronic conductivity of the Ta-SnO2 support due to higher carrier concentration and its intrinsic oxidation resistance [6]. More recently, we reported the stabilizing effect of the Nb-doped SnO2 support (Nb-SnO2) for the ORR on Pt nanorod catalysts [18]. A platinum anti-dissolution mechanism (PADM) for Pt/Nb-doped SnO2 cathode catalyst layers was also proposed to enhance the load cycle durability under the PEFC operating conditions [19]. In order to promote the ORR kinetics, alloying Pt with nonprecious metals such as Cr, Fe, Co, Ni, Cu, etc. has been recognized as one of the very effective strategies[20–22]. It was also demonstrated that, in an acidic electrochemical environment, a Pt-skin like surface was formed on the alloy by leaching of the surface nonprecious metal atoms [23,24]. The electronic state of the Pt skin is modified by the underlying alloy, which contributes to the enhanced ORR activity in comparison with that for pure Pt [25,26]. Despite great advances in this field, the construction of Pt alloy catalysts on metal oxide support has been relatively unexplored in comparison to that for pure Pt catalyst. Also, importantly, the ORR activity should be characterized in view of the operation temperature of the PEFC, e.g., 60-90 °C for fuel cell vehicles (FCVs) and residential co-generation systems. Investigating the temperature dependence would help to identify the proper operation temperature and provide fundamental aspects of the ORR catalysis, especially information about the reaction activation energy which is concerned with the adsorption/dissociation/desorption of chemically reactive species, together with the electron transfer. This mechanistic understanding would help inform the development of more active and durable ORR electrocatalysts. To the best of our knowledge, such thermodynamic studies for alloy catalysts supported on metal oxides have not been forthcoming. In the present work, in order to impart improved catalytic activity and durability, we constructed a PtCoSn alloy nanoparticle catalyst supported on Ta-SnO2 (PtCoSn/Ta-SnO2). The ORR activity of PtCoSn/Ta-SnO2 electrocatalyst was examined in 0.1 M HClO4 solution at temperatures ranging from 20 to 80°C. Also, new mechanistic insights were sought concerning the manner in which the synergistic interaction between Pt, Co and Sn boosts the ORR kinetics, based on density functional theory (DFT) calculations. Furthermore, we conducted a preliminary durability test by a potential-step cycling (0.6↔1.0 V) protocol at 80°C, which simulates frequent load variations in the operating FCV.

The Ta-SnO2 support (Ta dopant content 3 atom %) with a fused-aggregate network microstructure was synthesized by the flame oxide-synthesis method, as previously described [27]. The Pt-Co nanoparticles were loaded by a modified colloidal method [28,29]. Briefly, reagent-grade chloroplatinic acid was dissolved in a sodium bisulfite aqueous solution with stirring. After dilution with ultrapure water (Milli-Q water, Millipore Japan Co., Ltd.), the pH was adjusted to 5 by use of sodium carbonate solution. Hydrogen peroxide was added at 2 mL min−1 to obtain a colloidal solution of Pt hydroxide nanoparticles. A cobalt chloride aqueous solution was mixed with the colloidal solution after adjusting the pH to 5.5. The Ta-SnO2 support powder were dispersed in water, and was then mixed with the colloidal solution at room temperature. The resulting mixture was stirred overnight at room temperature, filtered, and thoroughly rinsed with ultrapure water to remove trace amounts of remaining chlorides. Finally, the obtained sample was heat-treated at 800°C in N2 atmosphere for 1 h, reduced at 150°C in 10% H2 (N2 balance) for 1 h, and quickly cooled to room temperature in the same atmosphere. The diffusion of Sn into Pt-Co particles was induced by strong metal-support interaction, as well as hydrogen-induced segregation. The resultant catalyst was denoted as PtCoSn/Ta-SnO2.

For purposes of comparison, two commercial catalysts provided by TKK (Tanaka Kikinzoku Kogyo, Japan), Pt catalyst supported on carbon black (c-Pt/C, TEC10E50E, 46.3 wt%-Pt) and Pt3Co catalyst supported on carbon black (c-Pt3Co/C, TEC36E52, 46.6 wt%-Pt, 5.2 wt%-Co), were used as received and subjected to the same electrochemical measurements.

The surface areas of the Ta-SnO2 support powder were estimated by the Brunauer-Emmett-Teller adsorption method (BET, Autosorb-iQ, Anton-Paar GmbH, Austria). The metal contents in the resultant catalyst were quantified by inductively coupled plasma-mass spectrometry (ICP-MS, 7500CX, Agilent Technologies Co. Ltd., USA). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images of the synthesized catalyst were taken using a H-9500 microscope (Hitachi High-Tech. Co., Japan). The elemental distribution was observed by scanning transmission electron microscope (STEM, HD-2700, Hitachi High-Tech. Co., Japan) with an energy-dispersive X-ray analyzer (EDX, Quantax XFlash 5700, Bruker Nano GmbH, Germany).

The electrochemical measurements were performed with a HZ-7000 potentiostat (Hokuto Denko Co., Japan) using the thin-layer rotating disk electrode (RDE) method [18]. The RDE cell was equipped with a hot water circulating system to control the temperature. A Pt mesh was used as the counter electrode and a reversible hydrogen electrode was used as the reference electrode. The working electrode was prepared as follows: the catalysts were coated onto the glassy carbon (GC) disc (diameter 5 mm) with a constant Pt loading of 12 μg cm−2, and then a suspension of 0.2 wt% Nafion (diluted with ethanol and water at 3:2 vol%) was pipetted on the electrode surface, yielding an average thickness of 0.05 μm. The thickness of the Nafion film was calculated based on its mass and the electrode surface area assuming a density of 1.98 g cm−3 in the dry state.

The electrolyte solution, 0.1 M HClO4, was prepared from reagent grade chemicals and ultrapure water. Cyclic voltammograms (CVs) were recorded in N2-saturated solution with a sweep rate of 50 mV s−1. The electrochemically active surface area (ECA) was evaluated from the hydrogen adsorption charge in the CV. Polarization curves for the ORR were recorded in O2-saturated electrolyte at 5 mV s−1 (positive potential scan) and 1750 rpm. The kinetic current I k at 0.90 V was calculated using the Koutecky-Levich equation. The I k was normalized to the ECA-based real surface area to generate specific activity j k, while mass activity MA k was obtained by dividing I k with the employed Pt mass on the GC electrode.

The density function theoretical (DFT) calculations were carried out by use of the DMol3 package (BIOVIA, Materials Studio, Version 2019, USA) with periodic boundary conditions. Further details are provided in the Supplementary Information.

Based on BET analysis, the specific surface area of the Ta-SnO2 support was determined to be approximately 37 m2 g−1. The Pt content in the PtCoSn/Ta-SnO2 was 16.9 wt%, while Co was 1.8 wt%. The contents of Sn and Ta were 61.4 wt% and 3.8 wt%, respectively. Fig. 1 a presents the TEM image, which reveals a clear contrast between the catalyst NPs and the Ta-SnO2 support, and the histogram of the particle size distribution is shown in Fig. S1, from which the average particle size was measured to be 8.7 ± 2.2 nm. The results of STEM and corresponding EDX mapping (Fig. 1b–f) confirm that the catalyst NPs are comprised of a Pt-Co-Sn alloy.

Fig. 2 a–c show cyclic voltammograms (CVs) for PtCoSn/Ta-SnO2, c-Pt3Co/C and c-Pt/C catalysts measured at different temperatures, respectively. These exhibit features corresponding to the hydrogen adsorption/desorption below 0.4 V and to the adsorption/desorption of oxygenated species above 0.6 V. The hydroxyl (OH) adsorption peaks for c-Pt/C shifted toward less positive potentials when the temperature increased, and the corresponding redox couple appears to become more reversible, approaching those for the Pt(111) single-crystal electrode (centred near 0.8 V). The increased reversibility at high temperature could reflect the enhanced electron transfer kinetics between the electrocatalyst and the OH adsorbate. A similar trend for potential shift with increasing temperature was also observed for PtCoSn/Ta-SnO2 and c-Pt3Co/C, while the peak potentials were more positive than those of c-Pt/C, e.g., at 60 °C (Fig. 2d), indicating that the adsorption of OH is weakened on PtCoSn/Ta-SnO2 and c-Pt3Co/C, or, the OH requires a higher potential to form, as well as being able to be reduced at a higher potential. The double layer or capacitive current was larger for the carbon support than that for Ta-SnO2, due to the higher surface area of the carbon (Ketjen black, ca. 875 m2 g−1). The ECA values, determined from the hydrogen adsorption charge, were around 25 m2 g−1 Pt for PtCoSn/Ta-SnO2, 40 m2 g−1 Pt for c-Pt3Co/C, and 80 m2 g−1 Pt for c-Pt/C and were not largely dependent on temperature, as shown in Table S1. The Pt utilization evaluated based on the measured ECA and the theoretical value (calculated based on the diameters of spherical catalyst particles) was comparably high for all three catalysts (70-80%), indicating that most of the catalyst particles were in contact the electrolyte solution.

Fig. S2 shows typical polarization curves for the ORR measured in O2-saturated 0.1 M HClO4 at various temperatures from 20 to 80 °C. Consistent with literature results [30], the diffusion-limiting current density (j L) is nearly constant up to a temperature of 60°C. The j L decreased significantly when the temperature was increased to 80 °C, which is due to a drastic decrease of the concentration of oxygen dissolved in the electrolyte solution. Fig. 3 a shows the ORR polarization curves at 20 °C, and the derived specific activity j k and mass activity MA k at 0.90 V are summarized in Fig. 3b. The PtCoSn /Ta-SnO2 exhibited a remarkable j k of 1.22 mA cm−2 Pt, which was 5.1 times higher than that of c-Pt/C and 1.7 times higher than that of c-Pt3Co/C. The enhancement of the catalytic activity was also higher than that of Pt nanorod supported on Nb-SnO2 [18], indicating that the use of Pt alloy is one of the essential strategies to enhance the ORR activity. The MA k of PtCoSn/Ta-SnO2 was also superior to c-Pt/C while being comparable with c-Pt3Co/C. Fig. 3c and d show the j k and MA k values at 0.90 V as a function of temperature. The j k value for c-Pt/C increased with increasing temperature and appeared to be levelling off at high temperature, > 60 °C, which is presumably because the increase in the intrinsic ORR rate (k app, as will be discussed below) with temperature was offset by the decrease in the concentration of O2. Differing from c-Pt/C, both c-Pt3Co/C and PtCoSn/Ta-SnO2 showed a clear decrease of j k at 80 °C, probably due to Co dissolution which would depress the ORR kinetics, together with the negative effect of the reduced oxygen concentration. Nevertheless, the j k value for PtCoSn/Ta-SnO2 was still larger than that for c-Pt/C and comparable with that of c-Pt3Co/C at 80 °C. The MA k values exhibited a similar trend to that for j k. In the low temperature range, 20-60 °C, the MA k values for PtCoSn/Ta-SnO2 were found to be comparable to those of c-Pt3Co/C while being larger than those for c-Pt/C. At 80 °C, the high j k value for PtCoSn/Ta-SnO2 was offset by the lower ECA, resulting in inferior MA k values compared to those for both c-Pt3Co/C and c-Pt/C.

In order to discriminate between the effects of kinetics and O2 concentration on the j k, we have evaluated the apparent rate constants k app for the ORR with the appropriate correction for the change in O2 concentration with temperature, according to the following equation [31],
(1)
I
k
/
( 4 FS )
=
k
app
[
H
+
]
[
O
2
]
where F is the Faraday constant, S is the Pt surface area determined by CV, [H+] is the bulk concentration of H+ (0.1 M), and [O2] is the concentration of O2 dissolved in the electrolyte solution at each temperature [32]. Fig. 4 a shows Arrhenius plots of k app at 0.90 V for various catalysts. At 20-60 °C, the k app values for PtCoSn/Ta-SnO2 increased with temperature, following a linear relationship between log k app and 1/T, corresponding to the Arrhenius equation [31]:
(2)
k
app
= Z exp
( −
E
a
/ RT )
and the k app values were larger than those for c-Pt/C and c-Pt3Co/C by factors of about 4.5 and 1.5, respectively. The k app values for PtCoSn/Ta-SnO2 tended to level off at 80 °C, nearly identical with that for c-Pt3Co/C. The curvature (slowing of k app increase) from 60 to 80 °C for PtCoSn/Ta-SnO2 and c-Pt3Co/C (less pronounced) might be the result of Co dissolution, as will be discussed below. An apparent activation energy E a in the temperature range of 20-60 °C (Fig. 4b) was found to be 19.7 kJ mol−1 (0.204 eV) for PtCoSn/Ta-SnO2, which is slightly lower than those for c-Pt/C (25.5 kJ mol−1, 0.264 eV) and c-Pt3Co/C (25.1 kJ mol−1, 0.260 eV). We consider that these relatively small differences in E a may not fully account for the large activity variation, especially when comparing c-Pt3Co/C with c-Pt/C. The high ORR activity on PtCoSn/Ta-SnO2 could also be attributed to a large preexponential factor Z in Eq. (2), as will also be discussed below.

After 80 °C operation, STEM-based EDX line analysis was performed to investigate the PtCoSn/Ta-SnO2 structures. Fig. S3 reveals the formation Pt-rich surface (Pt skin) on PtCoSn alloy for catalyst NPs, which is likely induced by leaching of Co under acidic and electrochemical conditions. It has been very important to investigate the effect of Co dissolution or Co content. In our previous work [29], we carefully varied the Co content in a series of PtCo/Ta-SnO2 catalysts from 0 to 33 at%, and we found an increase of ORR activity with increasing Co content, with the highest activity being obtained at 25 at%, which can correlate with the modified electronic structures of the Pt skin surface, due to electron transfer from the underlying Co [33]. On the other hand, a substantial dissolution of Co from Pt-Co alloy would weaken the electronic promotion effect and thus lead to a decay of the ORR specific activity [34]. Moreover, it was found from electron diffraction (ED) patterns that potential cycling-induced dissolution of Co resulted in rearrangement of the crystal structure of a Pt skin/PtCo alloy catalyst (converted to single-phase fcc), which affected both the activity and durability [35]. In order to obtain deeper insights into these effects for PtCoSn/Ta-SnO2, we will design PtSn/Ta-SnO2 as a reference sample and conduct in situ X-ray absorption spectroscopy (XAS) combined with an electrochemical cell to probe both the electronic and structural properties under reaction conditions, which will be reported in the near future.

To gain fundamental insight into mechanisms responsible for the excellent ORR activity of PtCoSn/Ta-SnO2 catalyst, especially at lower temperatures, we performed DFT calculations. We examined the possible role in the ORR of the Sn as an alloying metal in the catalyst NPs, as well as that of Co. Several recent papers have reported experimental and computational studies of PtSn-relevant catalysts for ORR [36–39]. We reported, based on hard X-ray photoelectron spectroscopic (HAXPES) measurements [36], that the involvement of Sn or the formation of a PtSn alloy along the metal-support interface can decrease the height of the Schottky barrier, facilitate electron transport, and impede the movement of catalyst particles. The electronic state of Pt was modified when alloying with Sn, showing dependence on the Pt/Sn composition, which could play a substantial role in boosting the ORR activity. Yan et al. reported lower initial ORR activity for PtSn nanorods but with better durability versus potential cycling than Pt [37]. They proposed that Sn replacing Pt in the surface layer can impede Pt oxidation. Su et al. found initial higher activity for PtSn nanoparticles than Pt at higher potentials (> 0.80 V vs. RHE) and also better durability versus cycling [38]. They proposed that the Pt3Sn present in the catalyst produced an electronic effect that enhanced the ORR activity, as discussed below. In a DFT study, Wang et al. also simulated a surface in which both Pt and Sn were present [39]. Electronic charge was found to be transferred from Sn to Pt. Decreased adsorption energies were found on the Sn-containing surface for ORR intermediates. However, the presence of Sn species on or in the surface can create an ambiguous situation, in our view, as it can exist as Sn, SnOH or SnO2. In the present work, we found that essentially no Sn species exists on the surface, based on the absence of an extra set of redox peaks between 0.60 and 0.80 V relating to Sn and/or SnOx in the CV (Fig. 2a and d), as well as the EDX line-scan result (Fig. S3). Thus, we have carried out calculations for models in which Sn exists only below the surface, influencing the kinetics via a ligand effect. A series of four Pt/Pt3CoxSn1-x(111) models was constructed to simulate the active sites in the PtCoSn/Ta-SnO2 catalyst (Fig. S4). The calculations were also carried out for a Pt/Pt3Co(111) model to understand the effect of the underlying Co, as well as Pt(111) for comparison. Fig. 5 shows that the water molecules initially participate by hydrogen bonding with the dissociating O2 molecule, yielding Oad and OHad, the latter being produced via protonation from the adjacent water. As depicted in Fig. 6 , the O2 adsorption energies were largest on the Pt/Pt3CoxSn1-x(111) models, in comparison to pure Pt(111), which, in turn, was larger than that on Pt/Pt3Co(111). The enhanced O2 adsorption and accompanying increase in the coverage correspond to the increased preexponential factor Z values in Eq. 2 mentioned above and thus the ORR activity, as suggested by Watanabe et al. [31]. Furthermore, the activation energies for the O2 dissociation assisted by two water molecules were found to be smallest on the Pt/Pt3CoxSn1-x(111) models (0.138 to 0.219 eV), as compared with Pt/Pt3Co(111) (0.327 eV) and Pt(111) (0.225 eV), consistent with the trend experimentally found, i.e., 0.204 eV for PtCoSn/Ta-SnO2, which was smaller than 0.260 eV for c-Pt3Co/C and 0.264 eV for c-Pt/C. It is noteworthy that, after the activation barrier for O2 dissociation has been reached on Pt(111), the products (shown as solid black squares in Fig. 6) immediately become 4OH, with strong adsorption, whereas, on all of the Pt/Pt3CoxSn1-x(111) models, the products are less strongly adsorbed, because they have not reached the 4OH stage, although they can also form 4OH on the surface (see solid symbols), but this does not appear to occur spontaneously, consistent with the more positive potentials of the OH adsorption peaks (Fig. 2d). The subsequent OH reduction would be also facile, as indicated by the more positive potentials of the OH desorption peaks (Fig. 2d).

The activation energy calculated by DFT on Pt/Pt3Co(111) was slightly larger than that on Pt(111), although the experimental result was that the activation energies on c-Pt3Co/C and c-Pt/C were nearly identical (as shown above). In addition, the kinetic enhancement on the c-Pt3Co/C catalyst in comparison with c-Pt/C appears to be based on a larger preexponential factor Z of Eq. 2. It has been considered that this is due to the more facile removal of adsorbed OH based on its weaker adsorption (see also Fig. 6), which could release more active sites for O2 adsorption, thereby increasing O2 coverage and thus the preexponential factor Z [40]. On the other hand, however, as seen in Fig. 6, the calculated O2 adsorption is also weak on Pt/Pt3Co(111), so that other factors might also be involved, as we are continuing to investigate. The present calculations suggest that the weak O2 adsorption on Pt with underlying Co can be offset by the strong O2 adsorption on Pt with even a small amount of underlying Sn. Thus, based on both experimental and DFT results, the latter for a wide range of Sn-Co ratios, we conclude that the facilitated adsorption and dissociation of O2, as well as easier OH reduction, afforded by the effects of alloying Pt with Sn and Co, is responsible for the remarkable ORR activity of the PtCoSn/Ta-SnO2 catalyst.

The catalyst durability was evaluated by an accelerated degradation test (ADT) using a potential-step cycling regime [0.6 V (3 s)-1.0 V (3s), 6 s for one cycle] at 80°C in N2-purged 0.1 M HClO4 aqueous solution. The CVs and ORR polarization curves before and after 4000 cycles of ADT are shown in Fig. 7 . The changes in ECA, ORR specific activity j k and mass activity MA k were summarized in Fig. 8 . After 4000 cycles, the ECA of c-Pt/C showed a significant drop of 43.2% (Fig. 8a), which is primarily caused by dissolution of Pt surface atoms and agglomeration of Pt particles via the electrochemical Ostwald ripening process. Since the j k remained practically constant, the change of MA k was synchronized with that of ECA. The essential criterion to be determined for catalytic stability is either the percentage of retention of activity or the percentage decrease of activity during a continuous test. For c-Pt3Co/C, while the decrease of ECA is small, the j k underwent a substantial loss of 34.8% (Fig. 8b) due to the dealloying of Co as reported previously [34]. The MA k of c-Pt3Co/C decreased by 31.1% from the initial value as a result of the ADT. In contrast, the PtCoSn/Ta-SnO2 exhibited remarkable durability, with absolutely no loss in the MA k after the ADT. Also, notably, the retained MA k after the ADT (Fig. 8c) for the PtCoSn/Ta-SnO2 catalyst was 1.62 and 1.13 times greater than that of c-Pt/C and c-Pt3Co/C, respectively. The enhanced durability of PtCoSn/Ta-SnO2 is presumably ascribed to the strong metal-support interactions (SMSI), as suggested in our recent HAXPES studies [36], as well as other works [16,27,28,41,42]. The SMSI could play a role in restraining Pt from dissolution, migration and agglomeration. In addition, as shown in our previous X-ray absorption spectroscopic (XAS) study [43], the Pt skin-covered alloy has a more stable, rigid structure than Pt with various adsorbates on the surface, so that little motion would be expected for the surface Pt atoms during the electrochemical processes. Such a rigid Pt skin could also contribute to achieving a great improvement in the ORR durability.

The PtCoSn nanoparticle catalysts supported on Ta-SnO2 are prepared. The ORR activity of PtCoSn/Ta-SnO2 catalyst has been characterized in 0.1 M HClO4 over a wide temperature range, 20-80°C. Due to an interplay between ORR kinetics and the oxygen concentration in the electrolyte solution, both specific activity and mass activity exhibit an optimum at ~60 °C. The ORR rate of PtCoSn/Ta-SnO2 increased with increasing temperature from 20 to 60 °C and was limited by the dissolution of Co at 80 °C. The PtCoSn/Ta-SnO2 maintained a higher ORR rate than that for commercial c-Pt3Co/C up to 60 °C, and, up to 80 °C, higher than that for a commercial Pt/C catalyst. The activity enhancement can be rationalized based on the Arrhenius equation and DFT calculations, which suggest a beneficial role for Pt alloyed with Co and Sn: O2 is expected to achieve a high coverage and facile dissociation on the active Pt surface sites. This beneficial synergistic behavior can be exploited in future work with purposefully designed catalysts. Although the initial activity at 80 °C was not outstanding for the PtCoSn/Ta-SnO2 catalyst, it showed superior durability. We are now working on the optimization of the preparation protocol for PtCoSn/Ta-SnO2 to further enhance the initial ORR activity at high temperature.

Guoyu Shi: Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Writing – original draft. Takuma Hashimoto: Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis. Donald A. Tryk: Investigation, Software, Writing – review & editing. Tetsuro Tano: Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis. Akihiro Iiyama: Formal analysis, Validation, Resources. Makoto Uchida: Formal analysis, Validation, Resources. Katsuyoshi Kakinuma: Conceptualization, Methodology, Validation, Resources, Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was partially supported by funds for the JSPS KAKENHI Grant Number 20H02839 from the Japan Ministry of Education, Culture, Sports, Science and Technology, and the project from the New Energy and Industrial Technology Development Organization (NEDO) of Japan.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.electacta.2021.138894.
